sourceSearches.notes: Use the official Pokedex page for species identity, typing, and official presentation.
Same pageview window as Mario pilot for comparable public attention.

identity.notes: Pikachu is both a game creature and a franchise/media mascot, so analysis must separate gameplay identity from brand identity.

relationshipsToTrack.name: Eevee
relationshipsToTrack.relationship: parallel mascot / version counterpart in Let's Go

roleAndNarrative.notes: Game data is official; anime/media relationship data needs separate source collection.

visualDesign.silhouette: small round mascot with long ears and lightning tail
visualDesign.notes: Visual coding is straightforward but should later cite official art references by generation or media type.

popularitySignals.notes: Attention proxy only. It does not capture global merchandise, anime, or game engagement.
Likely top-tier recognition because Pikachu is a global Pokemon brand representative.

communityAndFanbase.notes: Pikachu needs a merchandise/media-specific layer because normal game-character metrics undercount its cultural footprint.

analysisUsefulness: Useful contrast to Mario because Pikachu is non-human and largely clothing-free.
Good test case for separating game data from cross-media brand data.
dataGaps: Need comparable community metrics across Pokemon and non-Pokemon characters.

normalizedCandidate.name: Pikachu
normalizedCandidate.summary: Pokemon's central mascot and an electric-type creature whose game, anime, merchandise, and brand presence make it one of the clearest top-tier character benchmarks.

popularitySignals.notes: Normalized against Mario's current pilot total only for temporary comparison.

visualTags.silhouette: small round mascot with long ears and lightning tail

analysisNotes: Compare against Mario, Sonic, and Pac-Man for mascot analysis.
Cross-media metrics are essential for fair Pikachu analysis.
